sections.header: Verses 1–12 — Joram of Israel; the Three Kings March; Water Crisis

heb.paragraph: Joram removes Ahab’s matztzevet habbaʿal — the standing stone of Baal (v.2). But the Deuteronomistic historian immediately adds: “yet he clung to the sins of Jeroboam.” Removing Baal is not enough; the golden calves remain. Half-measures are no measures.

refs.note: Jehoshaphat asks “Is there no longer a prophet of the LORD?” — the identical question he asks here. His faithfulness is consistent.
David conquered Moab. Moab’s rebellion shows how far Israel has fallen from Davidic power.

notes.note: When their own foolishness creates a crisis, the impenitent blame Providence. Those who refuse to seek God before the crisis have no right to accuse him during it.
Elisha’s willingness to help for Jehoshaphat’s sake demonstrates corporate solidarity — the faithful benefit the unfaithful by their presence.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: nōqēd — a breeder of sheep. The same rare word describes Amos (Amos 1:1). Mesha’s tribute of 100,000 lambs represents enormous pastoral wealth. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

yātsaq mayim ʿal-yĕdē — “poured water on the hands of” — an idiom for personal service as attendant. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Mesha Stele (Moabite Stone), discovered 1868 at Dhiban, confirms this chapter. King Mesha describes the same rebellion from Moab’s perspective: “Omri king of Israel oppressed Moab for many days.” One of the most important extra-biblical inscriptions.
The circuitous march around the Dead Sea — approximately 100 miles through the wilderness of Edom — was a standard strategy to avoid Moab’s heavily fortified northern border.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Joram’s partial reform illustrates the northern kingdom’s persistent problem: even its best kings cannot break with the foundational sin of the divided monarchy.
Elisha’s response — “I would not even look at you” to Joram but “I have respect for Jehoshaphat” — shows that legitimate kingship matters.

hist.context: After Ahab’s death, Moab rebels. Joram enlists Jehoshaphat and Edom for a joint campaign through the wilderness south of the Dead Sea. The army runs out of water after seven days. Joram blames YHWH; Jehoshaphat asks for a prophet. The contrast is sharp: Joram sees God as the problem, Jehoshaphat sees God as the solution — echoing the same dynamic in 1 Kings 22.

sections.header: Verses 13–27 — Elisha’s Prophecy; Water in the Valley; Mesha’s Child Sacrifice

heb.paragraph: After Mesha sacrifices his firstborn son on the wall, “great wrath” (qetzef gadōl) came against Israel (v.27). Whose wrath? The narrator leaves it deliberately ambiguous — the horror of child sacrifice produces a response so overwhelming that Israel withdraws despite military advantage.

refs.note: Moses struck the rock and water came forth — God provides water in the wilderness. Elisha’s miracle operates on the same pattern.
“Do not give any of your children to be sacrificed to Molek.” Child sacrifice is the ultimate covenant violation.
Saul encountered prophets with harps, and the Spirit came upon him. Music and prophecy are associated throughout the OT.

notes.note: God commands digging before sending water — he works through means. Faith and effort are not opposed. We pray for rain and dig the channels.
The sacrifice reveals the demonic logic of idolatry at its extreme. The progression from superstition to human sacrifice is not a departure from idolatry but its natural conclusion.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: kĕnaggen hamĕnaggen — “as the musician played.” The “hand of the LORD” (yad-YHWH) is a standard expression for prophetic inspiration (cf. 1 Kgs 18:46; Ezek 1:3).
qetzef gadōl — “great wrath.” The subject is unstated, creating deliberate ambiguity. Proposals include divine anger, Chemosh’s anger (reported without endorsement), or Israel’s own revulsion.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Music accompanying prophetic activity is well attested in the ANE. Egyptian texts describe temple musicians who induce ecstatic states. The biblical text distinguishes Israel’s prophecy by grounding it in the “hand of the LORD” — a personal divine action.
The Mesha Stele confirms the military details from Moab’s perspective. Mesha records: “Israel has perished forever” — a premature obituary. The stele also confirms Chemosh worship and herem warfare.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The phrase “this is an easy thing” redefines the narrative scale. The prophetic perspective constantly reframes human events in divine terms.
The narrator’s refusal to explain the “great wrath” is itself a theological statement. The withdrawal is not military defeat but moral collapse before unspeakable evil.

hist.context: Elisha calls for a harpist; as the music plays, the hand of the LORD comes upon him and he prophesies: dig ditches and the valley will fill with water without rain. The prophecy is fulfilled at dawn. The Moabites, seeing sun reflected red in the water, mistake it for blood and charge into an ambush. Israel devastates Moab. But at the final siege, King Mesha sacrifices his firstborn son on the wall. “There came great wrath against Israel,” and they withdraw. The chapter ends with a theological shudder: the narrator does not explain the mechanism; he lets the horror speak for itself.

ppl.name: Elisha
ppl.text: Elisha is a key figure in The Campaign Against Moab.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: The contrast is sharp: Joram sees God as the problem, Jehoshaphat sees God as the solution — echoing the same dynamic in 1 Kings 22…

ppl.text: as the music plays, the hand of the LORD comes upon him and he prophesies: dig ditches and the valley will fill with water without rain…

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: “There came great wrath against Israel,” and they withdraw…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVNow Mesha king of Moab raised sheep, and he had to pay the king of Israel a tribute of a hundred thousand lambs and the wool of a hundred thousand rams.ESVNow Mesha king of Moab was a sheep breeder, and he had to deliver to the king of Israel 100,000 lambs and the wool of 100,000 rams.NIV“By what route shall we attack?” he asked. “Through the Desert of Edom,” he answered.ESVThen he said, “By which way shall we march?” Jehoram answered, “By the way of the wilderness of Edom.”
